turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 15-797, Moore versus Texas.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: For intellectually disabled persons, the death penalty would be cruel punishment in violation of the Eighth Amendment. This case concerns the way that Texas Court of Criminal Appeals determines whether a person convicted of a capital crime is intellectually disabled. The Texas Court of Criminal Appeals which I will refer to as the CCA is the state's highest court in criminal matters. Bobby James Moore was convicted of capital murder and sentenced to death for fatally shooting a store clerk during a botched robbery. At the time of the crime, Moore was 20 years old. A state habeas court later determined that, under this Court's decisions in Atkins v. Virginia and Hall against Florida, Moore qualified as intellectually disabled. The court so ruled after appraising Moore's intellectual functioning deficits and his adaptive deficits and finding the onset of these deficits while Moore was still a minor. Moore's IQ score is averaging roughly 70, the Habeas Court determined, placed him in the mild intellectually disabled category. Based on testimony from mental health professionals, the court further found that Moore had significant adaptive deficits. In line with these findings, the Habeas Court recommended to the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals that Moore be granted relief. The CCA disagreed, faulting the Habeas Court for failing to adhere to the CCA's 2004 decision in Ex Parte Briseno. In Briseno, the CCA employed standards for assessing intellectual disability contained in the 1992 edition of a clinical manual, a publication twice revised post 1992. Without citation to any medical or judicial authority, Briseno set out seven evidentiary factors that the CCA deemed relevant to the intellectual disability inquiry. Based on two of Moore's IQ scores, 74 and 78, the CCA concluded that Moore had not shown intellectual functioning deficits. Even if he had, the CCA continued his adaptive strengths offset any adaptive weaknesses. Briseno's seven evidentiary factors CCA added weighed against finding Moore intellectually disabled. We vacate the CCA's judgment because it disapproved the Habeas Court's employment of current medical guidance and reaffirm Briseno. The CCA's decision conflicts with the Eighth Amendment and this Court's past marking precedents, Atkins and Hall. The Eighth Amendment, those opinions hold, prohibits the execution of any intellectually disabled individual while Atkins and Hall left some leeway to the states in enforcing the ban on executing intellectually disabled persons. A state's discretion is not boundless. It must be informed, we instructed in Hall, by the medical community's diagnostic framework. Being informed by the medical community, we have noted, does not demand adherence to everything stated in the latest medical guide but neither does our precedent license disregard of current medical standards. The CCA's conclusion with respect to Moore's IQ scores is irreconcilable with Hall. There, we caution that when an IQ score is close to but above 70, courts must account for the test's imperfections, its standard error of measurement. Moore's IQ score of 70 had a range of 69 to 79. Because the low-ended range fell at or just below 70, the CCA had to move on to consider Moore's adaptive functioning. The CCA's consideration of Moore's adaptive functioning also deviated from prevailing clinical standards even from the older clinical standards the CCA deemed applicable. For example, the CCA concluded that Moore's record of academic failure along with a history of childhood abuse and suffering detracted from a finding of disability. In contrast, the medical community regards traumatic experiences of the kind Moore encountered not as detracting from but as risk factors for intellectual disability. The CCA's attachment to the seven Briseno evidentiary factors impeded its assessment of Moore's adaptive functioning because the Briseno factors create an unacceptable risk that a person with intellectual disability will be executed. The CCA erred in clinging to them. Other states do not use the Briseno factors in handling intellectual-disability pleas and Texas itself does not follow Briseno in context other than the death penalty. Because Briseno pervasively infected the CCA's analysis, the decision of that court cannot stand. For these reasons and others developed in the opinion, the judgment of the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals is vacated and the case is remanded for further proceedings not inconsistent with this opinion. The Chief Justice has filed a dissenting opinion joined by Justices Thomas and Alito.
